speaker: Jenny Wen
text: This design process that designers have been taught, where you go off and do a bunch of research and discovery, and then you diverge, converge, diverge, converge — we sort of treated as gospel. That's basically dead.
summary: The traditional design process of diverge-converge cycles that designers treated as gospel is now dead in the age of AI.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: There are basically two types of design work now. The first is supporting implementation and execution — engineers are using their seven Claudes to create features. The second is creating the vision or direction, but now it's a three to six month vision, not a two to five year one, and it's sometimes just creating a prototype that points people in the right direction.
summary: Design work has stratified into two modes: supporting fast engineering execution and setting short-term vision through prototypes rather than beautiful long-term decks.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: It's not just designers who are feeling like, 'Oh yeah, we have to keep up with engineers.' I think even engineers are like, 'How do we keep up with ourselves?'
summary: The pace of AI-driven engineering change is so fast that even engineers are struggling to keep up with their own capabilities.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: The way you really lose trust around quality and releasing something early is if you release it early and then nothing ever happens. But whenever you put something out early, it's possible to maintain the brand of your company if you commit to iterating, responding to feedback, and continuously shipping improvements.
summary: Shipping early is fine for brand trust as long as you visibly iterate and respond to user feedback — what kills trust is releasing something early and then going silent.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: I think AI will get better at taste and judgment and design. We might be holding onto that a little bit too much. At the end of the day, someone has to decide what is actually going to get built and what actually matters. Someone still needs to be accountable for the decision.
summary: AI will improve at taste and design judgment, but humans will still be needed for accountability and deciding what actually matters to build.

speaker: Jenny Wen
text: Being an IC across this past year gave me so many hard skills I wouldn't have gained if I was just managing. The design process has changed so much that I think design managers need to move back into IC work to truly understand what is happening, so they can be better managers.
summary: Design managers should rotate back into IC work to build empathy and hard skills for the rapidly changing AI-era design process.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: You have to know the difference between a megatrend and a hype cycle. When there's a megatrend, don't fight it. AI is a megatrend, one of the most foundational movements that we have seen in human history.
summary: Leaders must distinguish megatrends from hype cycles — AI is a megatrend you should never fight.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Every management book says praise in public, criticize in private. I fundamentally disagree. Establish enough trust so you are comfortable critiquing and debating in public. In private, build that trust. In public, you don't want posturing — you want problem-solving.
summary: Reverse the conventional wisdom: critique and debate in public for problem-solving, build trust and reassurance in private.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Don't delegate the storytelling. The story is not a marketing exercise after you built the product. The story is why you build the product. If you have seven or eight layers between you and the front line, you can't play the telephone game. Always own telling the story yourself.
summary: CEOs should never delegate company storytelling — the story defines why you build, and telephone-game message loss across org layers guarantees failure.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: The six things you need to build a great company, in descending order: timing, market, team, product, brand, distribution. You don't have all six, you don't win. A great team in a bad market gets dragged down — the market always wins.
summary: Great companies need six elements in priority order: timing, market, team, product, brand, and distribution — the market always trumps the team.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Permission to play means asking: is this logical for us? Do we have the route to market for mass scale distribution? If you dissipate your caloric burn across too many areas, nothing gets enough girth to drive all the way through. Be extremely selective where you expend your calories.
summary: Companies should only enter markets where they have permission to play and a distribution advantage.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: Persistence beats intellect, and stamina beats intellect any day of the week, twice on Sunday. The biggest reason successful leaders win is not that they're the smartest — it's that they have enormous staying power in the game.
summary: Stamina and persistence matter more than intelligence for leadership success.

speaker: Jeetu Patel
text: The platform that you choose and the quality of problems that you pick to solve determine a lot of the path of success for you. Harder problems attract better people, and business is a team sport.
summary: Choose hard problems to attract the best talent — easy problems won't draw the team you need to win.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: 100% of my code is written by Claude Code. I have not edited a single line by hand since November. Every day, I ship 10, 20, 30 pull requests.
summary: Boris has fully transitioned to AI-written code, shipping 10-30 PRs daily without manual editing.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: Productivity per engineer has increased 200%. I have never enjoyed coding as much as I do today, because I don't have to deal with all the minutia.
summary: 200% productivity increase from AI coding tools, with higher job satisfaction due to reduced tedious work.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: In a year or two, it's not going to matter. Coding is virtually solved. I imagine a world where everyone is able to program, anyone can just build software any time.
summary: Predicts coding will be virtually solved in 1-2 years, enabling universal software creation.

speaker: Boris Cherny
text: Claude is starting to come up with ideas. It's looking for feedback, it's looking at bug reports, it's looking at telemetry for bug fixes, and things to ship. A little more like a coworker.
summary: AI coding agents are evolving from tools to autonomous coworkers that identify and ship improvements independently.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: I look for four things in CEOs — my LOCK algorithm. L is for lovable — can I envision following this person? O is obsession with the problem. C is chip on the shoulder. K is deeply knowledgeable about the domain. And I'd add S for student — the best ones are deep, deep students of the game.
summary: The LOCKS framework for evaluating CEOs: Lovable, Obsessed, Chip-on-shoulder, Knowledgeable, and Student of the game.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: Starting a company has never been easier. The flip side is the number of companies formed is going to mushroom. There's so much noise and competition, it's just going to be hard to stand out and really accelerate and scale. It's never been easier to start, never been harder to scale.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: If you want to kill a plant, have two people water it. Every CEO at the adults' table has gone through this and they are religious about the DRI — directly responsible individual.
summary: Shared ownership kills execution at scale — every important initiative needs a single DRI.

text: EV is enterprise value, TV is your team's value, MEV is your value. Immature managers solve for TV over EV. We always put on the wall: solve for CV, then EV, then TV, then MEV. Customers first, then company, then team, then yourself.
summary: The priority hierarchy for decisions should be Customer Value, Enterprise Value, Team Value, then My Value.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: I always valued optionality, but there's a massive tax in optionality when you can move this fast. Planning cycles used to be a year, now they're three months.
summary: In the AI era, optionality is expensive — planning cycles have shrunk from annual to quarterly.

speaker: Brian Halligan
text: The go-to-market is going to get turned on its head. People start evaluating products in Gemini or ChatGPT. Your website is a lot less important. Sites will change to have a high quality avatar that knows everything about your products.
summary: Go-to-market will be transformed as buyers research in AI chatbots instead of websites.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: IC engineers are becoming tech leads. They're managing fleets and fleets of agents. I know many engineers on my team have 10 to 20 threads being pulled on at the same time.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: The Mythical Man-Month predicted software engineering would go where engineers are like surgeons. As a manager, look around corners and unblock people, especially from organizational bottlenecks.
summary: Managers should treat engineers like surgeons — proactively removing organizational blockers.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: The models will eat your scaffolding for breakfast. Build for where the models are going, not where they are today.

speaker: Sherwin Wu
text: We live in a bubble in Silicon Valley. A lot of work that runs our entire economy is not open-ended knowledge work — it's repeatable business processes with standard operating procedures.
summary: Business process automation is a massively underrated AI opportunity.

text: Every startup that I've seen fizzle out is not because OpenAI or a big lab has come and squashed them. It's because they built something that didn't resonate with customers.

speaker: Dr. Becky Kennedy

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: The market is immutable. No amount of tweeting, advertising is going to change whether the market wants your product.
summary: Product-market fit is like drug receptor binding — the market wants your product or it does not.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: Point solutions don't have enough data in the age of AI to be useful. If you own the mine with the data, you can make money.
summary: In the AI era, point SaaS solutions lack sufficient data context — only companies owning first-party data capture value.

speaker: Matt MacInnis
text: The purest form of ambition and most intense source of energy in the business is the founder CEO. Every next concentric circle of management has the potential to be an order of magnitude drop off in intensity.

speaker: Elena Verna
text: The best growth teams I've seen all have one thing in common: they measure retention before acquisition. If your bucket is leaky, pouring more water in doesn't help.

speaker: Elena Verna

summary: Good gamification makes progress visible through mechanics like leaderboards and streaks, not superficial badges.

speaker: Elena Verna

speaker: Edwin Chen

speaker: Edwin Chen

text: There's actually three levels to listening. Level one is internal. Level two is focused. Level three is global listening — you hear what they're communicating, not just saying.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: I use a GROW model. G is goal, R is reality, O is options, W is way forward.

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: We operate in tech. We're supposed to give all of ourselves purely logically. That's not at all true. The goal of any conflict is to create mutual understanding.

speaker: Rachel Lockett

speaker: Rachel Lockett
text: Would you enthusiastically rehire this person for the same role? That's the question we always asked at Stripe.

summary: Founders must create both the product and the market, positioning through familiar concepts.

speaker: Shaun Clowes
text: In everything always talk from the customer's perspective, from the market's perspective, from the competitor's perspective. The very small number of PMs do that. They get dragged into internal politics, scrum management.
summary: The best PMs consistently frame everything from customer, market, and competitor perspectives rather than getting dragged into internal politics and scrum management.

speaker: Shaun Clowes
text: The way AI will most impact product management is data management. If you don't have your data house in order, your AI products will be garbage. Data is the moat, not the model.
summary: AI's biggest impact on product management will be through data management because the quality of your data, not your model, determines product quality.

speaker: Shaun Clowes
text: If we were doctors, you'd be like, 'That's totally unacceptable.' Imagine if after 20 years of medical practice, most doctors still couldn't reliably diagnose common conditions.
summary: The low bar for PM competency would be unacceptable in fields like medicine, highlighting how much the discipline still needs to mature.

text: I'm throwing everything against the wall. I'm looking at what's going to stick. I am looking to try to find the winning tactic and turn it into a winning strategy.
summary: Benioff's approach to launching Salesforce was relentless experimentation, throwing everything at the wall and turning winning tactics into strategy.

summary: Thawar managed 120 direct reports by replacing traditional management with systems like pair programming and product backlogs for unblocking.

summary: Thawar's life motto 'everything you know is wrong' drives constant experimentation and first-principles thinking.

text: Test everything. We've run in the last four years over 600 experiments on the streaks, so every other day. We've actually set up really good infrastructure for copy testing.
summary: Duolingo runs an experiment on streaks every other day, totaling 600+ experiments in four years, with dedicated copy testing infrastructure.

summary: Changing a single CTA from 'continue' to 'commit to my goal' was a massive win, demonstrating the power of psychologically resonant copy.

speaker: Jackson Shuttleworth

summary: Duolingo frames its core challenge as habit formation, not language teaching, with next-day retention as the most important metric.

speaker: Jackson Shuttleworth
text: Loss aversion is way more powerful than reward seeking. The fear of losing your streak is more motivating than any badge or reward we could give you.

speaker: Seth Godin
text: AI very soon is going to stop being a feature the same way electricity is not a feature. What AI companies and all companies need to do is say, what's in this for the user? What promise do I want to make, a difficult promise, a remarkable promise, and then how do I keep it?
summary: AI will stop being a differentiating feature like electricity stopped being one; companies must focus on the promise they make to users, not the technology.

speaker: Seth Godin
text: I define good taste as knowing what other people want just before they do. People are seen as having good taste when they bring something to the world that the world didn't necessarily expect but is glad to see.
summary: Good taste is the ability to anticipate what people want before they know it themselves, delivering the pleasantly unexpected.

speaker: Seth Godin
text: Quality is not luxury. Quality is not perfection. Quality means meeting spec, and if you meet spec, you're done. If you don't think the spec is good enough, make a better spec.
summary: Quality means meeting spec, not perfection; if the spec feels insufficient, the answer is to raise the spec, not to endlessly polish.

speaker: Seth Godin
text: If you don't build the network effect into what you are making, you are almost certainly going to fail. The question is, will this work better for my users if they tell other people about it?
summary: Software products that do not have network effects built in from the start are almost certainly going to fail.

speaker: Seth Godin
text: When you choose your smallest viable audience, what language they speak, how much money they have, what problem they're trying to solve, you have chosen everything that's going to go into the product and what your future is going to be like.
summary: Choosing your smallest viable audience is the most consequential product decision because it determines everything about what you build.

speaker: Seth Godin
text: I have a very emotional connection to Claude.ai. I think they have a brand. ChatGPT's reputation with me is not good because it regularly over promises and under delivers and it does it without kindness or humility.
summary: Godin sees Claude as having a genuine brand built on kindness and humility, while ChatGPT over-promises and under-delivers.

speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
text: To be a founder is a state of being, it's an attitude. To be a CEO is a craft. The more founders who can accept that those are two separate things and they're both equally important to build an ascendant startup, the better all of us will be.
summary: Being a founder is a mindset while being a CEO is a learnable craft, and recognizing these as separate skills is crucial for startup success.

speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
text: Founder mode gets me angry. That article just got me hot. It really felt like an excuse. We were giving founders permission to not learn the job.
summary: Lowenhar sees the 'founder mode' concept as a dangerous excuse that gives founders permission to avoid learning the craft of being CEO.

speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
text: There are these two phases to a startup journey. Phase one is finding product-market fit. Phase two is building the company. Most founders focus entirely on phase one and never learn how to do phase two.
summary: Most founders obsess over finding product-market fit but never learn the equally critical second phase of actually building the company.

speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
text: The greatest CEOs know when to calibrate which one is needed. It's not manager mode is bad, it's that you need both modes and you need to know when to deploy each.
summary: Great CEOs do not choose between founder mode and manager mode; they calibrate between both based on what the situation demands.

speaker: Jonathan Lowenhar
text: The founders who scale are the ones who realize that their job description changes every six months. What got you here won't get you there, and that's not a failure, it's the nature of the journey.
summary: Founders who scale successfully accept that their job description fundamentally changes every six months as the company grows.

speaker: Amjad Masad
text: The idea behind Replit is that making software today is very difficult. We want to make it easier. People view this as a developer in their pocket essentially. We have 34 million users globally.
summary: Replit's mission is to democratize software creation, serving as a developer-in-your-pocket for 34 million global users.

speaker: Amjad Masad
text: Typically, you're bottlenecked where your ideas are not fitting in because they need to be made and they need to be made quickly. Now, you open up that bottleneck. So now actually making things is a lot easier. Actually, you become limited by how fast you can generate ideas.
summary: AI removes the implementation bottleneck in product development, shifting the constraint from building speed to idea generation speed.

speaker: Amjad Masad
text: I could imagine whatever five years from now, someone running a billion-dollar company with zero employees where it's like the support is handled by AI, the development is handled by AI, and you're just building and creating this thing.
summary: Masad envisions billion-dollar companies run by a single person within five years, with AI handling support, development, and operations.

speaker: Amjad Masad
text: The evals are everything. You can't ship an AI feature without a way to measure whether it's actually good. We built an entire internal evaluation infrastructure before we shipped our agent.
summary: Replit built comprehensive internal evaluation infrastructure before shipping their AI agent because evals are the foundation of AI product quality.

speaker: Amjad Masad
text: The best way to build an AI product is to use it yourself constantly. Our team uses Replit Agent to build Replit. That feedback loop is the fastest way to improve.
summary: Replit uses its own AI agent to build Replit itself, creating the tightest possible feedback loop for product improvement.

speaker: Raaz Herzberg
text: I was hired as the first product manager. I sat in on those calls. I still did not exactly understand what we were going to build, which was confusing. And that ended up pivoting us around to cloud security.
summary: Wiz's first PM joined before the product idea was figured out, and her confusion about what to build actually catalyzed the pivot to cloud security.

speaker: Raaz Herzberg
text: We would have 10 to 15 meetings every day with potential customers. At the time, we didn't really have a solid product yet.
summary: Wiz conducted 10-15 customer meetings daily before having a product, using extreme customer proximity to find product-market fit.

speaker: Raaz Herzberg
text: We went from zero to $100 million ARR faster than any SaaS company in history. But it wasn't luck. It was the combination of a team that had built and sold a company together before, and a market that was screaming for a solution.
summary: Wiz's record-breaking growth from zero to $100M ARR came from a team with prior exit experience targeting a market with extreme urgency.

speaker: Raaz Herzberg

summary: Wiz deliberately sold to CISOs rather than security engineers because executive buyers commit to deployments, not pilots.

speaker: Raaz Herzberg
text: Our product strategy is simple: we want to be the operating system for cloud security. Every new product we build has to connect to the graph. That's our moat.
summary: Wiz's moat is its connected security graph, and every new product must integrate into it, creating compounding value.

speaker: Archie Abrams
text: When you have teams naturally break up the world into different funnel stages, it gets very seductive to look at my part of the funnel and what's my conversion rate. summary: Funnel-stage teams are tempted to optimize their conversion rate by making the prior step harder, which is counterproductive to overall growth.

speaker: Archie Abrams
text: The best way to get more people to get to a step is just get more people in the door in the first place. That will always hurt your conversion rate, but it may actually give you more people on the outside.
summary: Widening the funnel top hurts conversion rates but produces more absolute outcomes, which matters more than percentages.

speaker: Archie Abrams
text: Shopify doesn't have KPIs in the traditional sense. We have a north star which is GMS, gross merchandise sales, and everything else is context. We trust people to make good decisions.
summary: Shopify avoids traditional KPIs in favor of a single north star metric (GMS) and trusts teams to make decisions with context.

speaker: Archie Abrams
text: We actually optimize for churn. Not to reduce it to zero, but to make sure we're acquiring the right merchants. If we reduce churn by making it hard to leave, we're just trapping bad-fit customers.
summary: Shopify optimizes for healthy churn rather than minimizing it, because trapping bad-fit merchants with exit barriers hurts long-term growth.

speaker: Archie Abrams
text: We build toward a 100-year vision. That sounds abstract but it changes every decision. You stop optimizing for this quarter's numbers and start building things that compound.
summary: Shopify's 100-year vision changes daily decisions by shifting focus from quarterly optimization to building compounding value.

text: Our jobs get frustrating when we behave, most of the time, in misalignment with our superpowers and who we truly are at our core. I have a preferred level at which I like to operate.
summary: Career frustration stems from spending most of your time operating at a level misaligned with your core superpowers and natural strengths.

speaker: Shreyas Doshi

summary: Pre-mortems surface hidden risks by asking teams to imagine project failure before starting, unlocking candid discussion of concerns.

speaker: Shreyas Doshi
text: The LNO framework: categorize every task as Leverage, Neutral, or Overhead. Do leverage tasks at 100%, neutral at 80%, and overhead at the minimum acceptable quality.
summary: The LNO framework categorizes tasks as Leverage, Neutral, or Overhead, with effort allocated proportionally to each category's impact.

speaker: Shreyas Doshi
text: Product work happens at three levels: impact, execution, and optics. Most execution problems are actually strategy problems. When you can't get alignment, it's because there's no clear strategy, not because people aren't executing.

summary: Impact-effort matrices create false rigor because truly high-impact work is almost never low-effort, making the framework misleading.

speaker: Shreyas Doshi
text: Thinking is cheap, so you should do more thinking, not less. The bias toward action in tech is overdone. Sometimes the highest leverage thing you can do is sit and think for two days before making a decision.

speaker: Anneka Gupta

summary: True strategic leaders combine articulating a clear vision with the courage to champion difficult changes -- one without the other falls short.

speaker: Tristan de Montebello
text: The games are meant to put you in a state of turbulence and find out what was easy, what was hard, what am I noticing? summary: Meaningful practice in public speaking comes from reps that push you to an edge of discomfort -- perfect reps teach you nothing.

speaker: Tristan de Montebello
text: It's so much more freeing, powerful, connecting, and effective to speak conversation.
summary: Conversational speaking is more powerful and connecting than polished presentation style -- it frees both the speaker and the audience.

text: Get clear on your objective function, and one way that I've gotten clear on it is trying to think about it from future me because five years from now, I'm not going to give a if I made the presentation slightly better, but I'm going to care a lot about what kind of relationship I have with my daughters.
summary: Clarify your life's objective function by asking what future-you will actually care about -- it reframes daily priorities away from trivial work tasks.

speaker: John Mark Nickels
text: Not to be morbid, but most of us just aren't really tuned into an awareness that our lives will come to an end. We try to pretend like we're going to live forever and just not think about it. summary: Accepting mortality is not morbid but clarifying -- it forces a genuine reprioritization of what actually matters in work and life.

speaker: Alex Komoroske
text: We've forgotten a world without aggregators. Aggregators make sense in the late stage of an era. At the beginning, they curtail too much exploration.

speaker: Alex Komoroske
text: Software is alchemy. It's the ability to extend human agency beyond ourselves to create something that can then combine with what others have created in unexpected and unforeseen ways. And somehow in the past decade, we've become convinced that all of this potential should be squeezed into about a dozen little boxes on your phone.
summary: Software should extend human agency and combine with others' creations in unforeseen ways, not be trapped inside monolithic apps controlled by a few companies.

speaker: Alex Komoroske
text: Do things that give you energy that you're proud of, and happiness is reality minus expectations I think are really simple clarifying words.
summary: Two clarifying life principles: pursue work that gives you energy and pride, and remember that happiness equals reality minus expectations.

speaker: Benjamin Lauzier
text: I think when you're running a marketplace, you tend to sit in your ivory tower a little bit, looking at stats and thinking like, 'If only we could get people to do X, it'd be better for everyone.' I think that's missing the point that we're humans.

speaker: Benjamin Lauzier
text: I'm a huge believer in market forces and empowerment, so provide guardrails for what a good experience is in your marketplace, set a clear bar for quality, and provide the right coaching and tools for supply to be successful, and then take a step back and see where the gaps are.

text: Make sure you go somewhere where you have a good engineering partner. Because if you have great ideas of what to build but you can't get them built, then you go nowhere. So that has to be part of your evaluation criteria that you meet and value your engineering partner before you join.

speaker: Eli Schwartz
text: Think of SEO as a product. The product managers are the people that should be thinking about this SEO question because it's a product question. summary: SEO should be treated as a product problem, not a marketing trick -- PMs should own it because it is about serving users on a self-discovery journey.

speaker: Eli Schwartz
text: Transparently, I thought this was going to be an apocalypse. Up until AI Overviews, whoever won on that long form piece of content would get that first click. But now that doesn't exist anymore.
summary: AI Overviews in search have fundamentally changed SEO by eliminating the first-click advantage that long-form content used to enjoy.

speaker: Eli Schwartz
text: In their documentation, they say that AI itself is not the problem. It's the helpfulness, the usefulness of the content that would be a problem.
summary: Google's stance on AI content is that AI authorship itself is not penalized -- what matters is whether the content is genuinely useful to users.

speaker: Camille Fournier
text: Hoarding credit. PMs, they tend to be the front-facing person for initiative. Engineers sometimes think that they don't get the credit for their work because the PM takes all the glory and all the credit for the project that they really worked very hard on.
summary: The number one thing PMs do that annoys engineers is hoarding credit -- being the front-facing person while engineers feel invisible for the real work.

speaker: Camille Fournier
text: The next thing that engineers really get annoyed about with PMs, when they just don't understand the details and act like they don't matter, it just shows a real lack of empathy for the work that engineers are doing and I think it really can be very off-putting.
summary: PMs who dismiss technical details signal a lack of empathy for engineering work, which is deeply off-putting to their engineering partners.

speaker: Phyl Terry
text: When you're looking for a job, you need a spear and not a net. What happens when we're building a product? Same thing, right? We want this product to be for everyone, but we've learned with product market fit that doesn't work. We need a narrow, clear focus.
summary: Job searching requires a spear, not a net -- just like product-market fit, you need narrow focus rather than trying to appeal to everyone.

speaker: Phyl Terry
text: While it's hard to figure out your candidate market fit, it's also a relief to know it's not about you. So what I ask people to do is I ask them to think about what they want and what they don't want. Now, you might not think that that's a radical step, but most people don't do that. When they get laid off, they spray and pray.
summary: Most people skip the fundamental step of defining what they want and do not want in a job -- instead they spray and pray, which rarely works.

speaker: Phyl Terry
text: There's no I in team. Well, there is an I in village, and the I in village is that when you start to interview and negotiate, you've got to be in charge. I want you to play to win, not not to lose.
summary: In job negotiations, shift from playing not-to-lose to playing to win -- take charge of the interview and negotiation process.

speaker: Sean Ellis
text: The question is, how would you feel if you could no longer use this product? Once you got a high enough percentage of users saying they'd be very disappointed, most of those products did pretty well. If you felt too low, those products tended to suffer.
summary: The Sean Ellis test -- asking users how disappointed they would be without the product -- reliably predicts product-market fit when 40%+ say very disappointed.

speaker: Sean Ellis
text: Just ignore the people who say they'd be somewhat disappointed. They're telling you it's a nice to have. summary: Focus on 'very disappointed' users, not 'somewhat disappointed' ones -- optimizing for nice-to-have feedback risks diluting the experience for your core fans.

speaker: Sean Ellis
text: It's usually much more function of onboarding to the right user experience than it is about the tactical things that people try to do to improve retention.
summary: Retention is driven more by onboarding users to the right experience than by tactical retention hacks.

speaker: Sean Ellis

summary: Sustainable pursuit of hard startup goals requires intentionally designing against burnout rather than treating it as an inevitable cost.

speaker: Kenneth Berger
text: The biggest sign is when you notice a gap between what you want and what you're asking for. You might want a promotion, but you're not saying it. You might want more resources, but you're hoping someone notices.
summary: The gap between what you want and what you explicitly ask for is the primary thing holding most people back in their careers.

speaker: Kenneth Berger
text: People think asking for what they want is selfish. But actually, when you're clear about what you need, you make it easier for everyone around you. Ambiguity is what creates conflict.
summary: Clearly stating your needs is not selfish but actually reduces conflict by eliminating the ambiguity that creates workplace tension.

speaker: Kenneth Berger
text: At Slack, one of the things I learned was that the best product decisions came from people who were willing to say what they actually thought, even when it was uncomfortable. The culture rewarded directness.
summary: Slack's best product decisions came from a culture that rewarded directness and people willing to voice uncomfortable truths.

speaker: Jag Duggal
text: We're not trying to be incrementally better, we are trying to be fundamentally different. We want our customers to love us fanatically.
summary: Nubank's core strategy is to be fundamentally different rather than incrementally better, aiming for fanatical customer love.

speaker: Jag Duggal
text: We built a lending product, we built an investment product, we built an insurance product, we built a series of small business products. We rarely scale a project until we know the Sean Ellis score hit a threshold that we find really compelling.
summary: Nubank uses the Sean Ellis score as a gate before scaling any new product line, ensuring strong product-market fit before investment.

speaker: Jag Duggal
text: Kevin Systrom in the early days of Instagram, I heard him say it at a conference. We may not be right, but at least we are clear. Even if your strategy isn't right, you have a very clear idea of what was supposed to be happening.
summary: Strategic clarity matters more than strategic correctness because a clear wrong strategy can be tested and corrected while an ambiguous one cannot.

speaker: Jag Duggal
text: One of the things we do at Nubank is we ask every product team to articulate what we call a 'magic moment.' What is the moment when the customer goes from 'this is interesting' to 'I love this'?
summary: Nubank requires every product team to define their 'magic moment' when customers cross from interest to love.

speaker: Jag Duggal
text: 80% to 90% of our growth is through word of mouth. That only happens if your product is fundamentally different. No one tells their friends about something that's slightly better. They tell their friends about something that blew their mind.
summary: Nubank's 80-90% word-of-mouth growth proves that viral organic growth only comes from products that are fundamentally different, not slightly better.

speaker: Vikrama Dhiman
text: I created a career growth framework for product managers, which comprises of three things. What you produce, what you bring to the table, and what's your operating model.
summary: PM career growth depends on three pillars: what you produce (artifacts), what you bring to the table (skills), and your operating model (how you work).

speaker: Vikrama Dhiman
text: Can you show me your last PRD? Can you show me the last product note that you sent? Can you show me the product strategy doc? You must have that impact through the artifacts that you work on.
summary: PM impact is demonstrated through tangible artifacts like PRDs and strategy docs, and early-career PMs should focus on producing them consistently.

speaker: Vikrama Dhiman
text: How you view change, whether you are focusing on things you control, and third is how you see yourself. The moment you are able to correct those stories, you may be back on the growth path again.
summary: Three mindset blocks impede career growth: how you view change, whether you focus on controllable factors, and your self-narrative.

speaker: Vikrama Dhiman
text: At Gojek, we had to build products for a market where people were coming online for the first time. You can't assume anything about user behavior. You have to go watch them use the product in their environment.
summary: Building products for emerging markets where users are coming online for the first time requires directly observing users in their real environment.

speaker: Vikrama Dhiman
text: The operating model is actually the most underrated part. How do you make decisions? How do you communicate? How do you handle ambiguity? These are the things that separate senior PMs from everyone else.
summary: A PM's operating model, covering decision-making, communication, and ambiguity handling, is the most underrated differentiator for senior-level growth.

speaker: Hamilton Helmer
text: Power requires a benefit and a barrier, so he's taking care of the benefit part by saying a castle, you have to have a pretty good understanding of why it's a castle and not a shack.
summary: True business power requires both a benefit (the castle) and a barrier (the moat); most companies have only one or neither.

speaker: Hamilton Helmer
text: You're on a treadmill and if you stop running on that treadmill, you get creamed, but it's not power. The things that drive operational excellence can be mimicked.
summary: Operational excellence is a treadmill, not a power; anything competitors can mimic does not constitute a durable strategic advantage.

speaker: Hamilton Helmer
text: There's a thing called power progression. There are times when certain types of power are available. The path to power is where the rubber meets the road.
summary: Different types of strategic power become available at different company stages, and timing the pursuit of power correctly is critical.

speaker: Hamilton Helmer
text: Network effects are the most powerful of the seven powers. If you have a product where every incremental user makes the product more valuable for every other user, that's an extraordinarily durable position.
summary: Network effects are the strongest of the seven powers because each new user increases value for all existing users, creating extraordinary durability.

speaker: Hamilton Helmer
text: Counter-positioning is when a newcomer adopts a new, superior business model that the incumbent can't adopt because it would damage their existing business. This is what Netflix did to Blockbuster.
summary: Counter-positioning occurs when a newcomer adopts a superior business model that incumbents cannot copy without damaging their existing business.

speaker: Annie Duke
text: It's so incredibly necessary in improving decision quality to take what's implicit and make it explicit. It's not that intuition is crap, your intuition is sometimes right. If you don't make it explicit, then you don't get to find out when it's wrong.
summary: Making implicit beliefs explicit is essential for improving decisions because it is the only way to discover when your intuition is wrong.

speaker: Annie Duke
text: People generally think the purpose of a meeting is for three things, discover, discuss, decide. The only thing that's ever supposed to happen in a meeting is the discussion part.
summary: Meetings should only be for discussion; discovery and decisions should happen before and after the meeting, respectively.

speaker: Annie Duke
text: So a pre-mortem, it's great only if you set up kill criteria. Commit to actions that you're going to take if you see those signals.
summary: Pre-mortems are only effective when paired with predefined kill criteria that commit teams to specific actions upon seeing warning signals.

speaker: Annie Duke
text: When you're making a decision where the outcome is very long-term, you need to focus on the quality of the process, not the quality of the outcome. Because the outcome is going to be affected by so many things you can't control.
summary: For long-term decisions, evaluate the quality of the decision process rather than the outcome since too many uncontrollable variables affect results.

speaker: Annie Duke
text: The most important thing about quitting is to set your criteria in advance. Decide when you will quit before you start. Once you're in it, your sunk cost bias will keep you going way too long.
summary: Setting quit criteria before starting a project is essential because sunk cost bias makes it nearly impossible to make rational quit decisions in the moment.

speaker: Kayvon Beykpour
text: We wanted to change the lack of ambition, the lack of creativity, the lack of customers feeling that the product had changed at all.
summary: Kayvon's mission at Twitter was to cure a stagnant culture where customers felt the product never changed and the team lacked ambition.

speaker: Kayvon Beykpour
text: The sacred cows are like their own roadmap. What are all the things that you think we're not allowed to change? Let's start there.
summary: Treating sacred cows as a roadmap for innovation by starting with the things everyone assumes cannot be changed.

speaker: Kayvon Beykpour
text: I was like, 'I might flame out completely, but Hell if I don't try.'
summary: Taking on a massive culture-change challenge requires accepting the possibility of total failure while refusing not to try.

speaker: Kayvon Beykpour
text: The number one thing that changed Twitter's shipping velocity was just getting people to believe they were allowed to ship. The culture had become so risk-averse that people self-censored their own ambition.
summary: Twitter's biggest shipping blocker was not technical but cultural: people self-censored their ambition due to extreme risk aversion.

speaker: Kayvon Beykpour
text: Live video taught me that you can build a product that people don't ask for and they don't know they want, and it can become something they can't live without. Periscope was that for me.
summary: Products people never asked for can become indispensable, as Periscope proved that live video was a latent need no one could articulate in advance.

speaker: Carole Robin
text: I feel that you don't care and I feel you're being insensitive are not feelings, and that's where we make our biggest mistakes when it comes to feedback.
summary: Statements like 'I feel you don't care' disguise judgments as feelings, and this confusion is the root of most feedback failures.

speaker: Carole Robin
text: Questions that start with what, when, where, how. Stay away from why.
summary: Asking 'why' triggers defensiveness; effective feedback uses questions starting with what, when, where, and how.

speaker: Carole Robin
text: We don't understand that we are only privy to two out of the three, so I know what's going on for me and I know what I did. I have no idea what happened on your end.
summary: The three realities framework reveals we only see our own intent and our actions, but are blind to the other person's experience.

speaker: Carole Robin
text: Anger is a secondary emotion. Really what's going on is you're afraid or you're hurt.
summary: Anger is always a secondary emotion masking fear or hurt, and addressing the root feeling transforms difficult conversations.

speaker: Carole Robin
text: The most robust relationships are the ones that have been through conflict and survived. If you've never had a hard conversation with someone, you don't actually have a deep relationship. You have a pleasant one.
summary: Deep relationships are forged through survived conflict, not pleasant avoidance; never having a hard conversation signals superficiality.

speaker: Mihika Kapoor
text: My take is that your scope is the world. Nothing should ever perceive as being out of bounds.
summary: The best PMs treat nothing as out of bounds, viewing their scope as the entire world rather than a narrowly defined product area.

speaker: Mihika Kapoor
text: We lean heavily into designing and prototyping even before a project gets a green light. If you and your team do your job correctly, what does the world look like?
summary: Building prototypes and designs before getting formal project approval creates compelling visions that make approval much easier to obtain.

speaker: Mihika Kapoor
text: We have this concept called Maker Week, which is our internal hackathon, giving people the breathing space to see ahead into the horizon and be wildly ambitious.
summary: Figma's Maker Week hackathon gives teams breathing space to think ambitiously beyond day-to-day work and explore future possibilities.

speaker: Mihika Kapoor
text: The way I think about hype is that it's a tool. You need internal hype to get resources and attention. But you have to back it up with substance. Hype without substance erodes trust very quickly.
summary: Internal hype is a necessary tool for securing resources and attention, but it must be backed by substance or it quickly erodes trust.

speaker: Mihika Kapoor
text: I think the best PMs are unreasonable. Not in a bad way, but they refuse to accept the constraints that everyone else accepts. They ask, what if we just didn't have that constraint?
summary: The best PMs are productively unreasonable, questioning accepted constraints and asking what would be possible without them.

speaker: Mihika Kapoor
text: One of the biggest shortcomings of AI is that it's optimized for the demo or optimized for the tweet. It's not really useful to enter a prompt and get an output that you can't interact with.
summary: Most AI products fail by optimizing for demo-worthy outputs rather than giving users the ability to interact with and manipulate results.

speaker: Dalton Caldwell
text: One of my mantras is just don't die. Being coached and being reminded of the fundamentals and basics puts you in the right mindset.
summary: The simplest and most important startup mantra is 'just don't die,' keeping founders focused on fundamentals over everything else.

speaker: Dalton Caldwell
text: Seems like an unsolved problem. You'll get all this positive feedback from the world and people have been starting that startup since the '90s.
summary: Tarpit ideas appear as unsolved problems with encouraging market feedback, but they have been trapping founders for decades without success.

speaker: Dalton Caldwell
text: A good pivot is like going home. It's warmer, it's closer to something that you're an expert at.
summary: The best pivots feel like coming home because they move founders closer to their domain expertise and authentic interests.

speaker: Dalton Caldwell
text: We're trying to mix up some of the information diet about what kind of ideas people might be contemplating they are currently.
summary: YC's request for startups is designed to broaden the idea diet of prospective founders beyond the narrow set of concepts they naturally encounter.

speaker: Dalton Caldwell
text: The founders who do best at YC are the ones who are resilient. They take the feedback, they iterate quickly, and they don't get emotionally attached to any single version of the product. They're attached to the problem.
summary: Top YC founders are resilient, iterate rapidly on feedback, and remain attached to the problem rather than any specific product iteration.

speaker: Todd Jackson
text: Finding product-market fit is the single most important thing that your startup does in the first three years, and it's just underexplored and it's just underexplained as a topic.
summary: Product-market fit is the single most important startup milestone yet remains surprisingly underexplored and poorly explained.

speaker: Todd Jackson
text: We've published dozens of articles on the First Round Review, and we have found a very consistent set of patterns, demand satisfaction, and efficiency. But the interesting thing is that you don't go for all three of them from the very beginning.
summary: Product-market fit follows three sequential patterns: demand, satisfaction, and efficiency, which should not all be pursued simultaneously.

speaker: Todd Jackson
text: You've got the persona, the problem, the promise, and the product. Lattice kept the first one but changed the others. Vanta changed all four.
summary: When stuck on product-market fit, the four Ps framework (persona, problem, promise, product) helps identify which elements to change.

speaker: Todd Jackson
text: Roughly, 60% are never going to get past L2.
summary: About 60% of startups never progress past the second level of product-market fit, making it a critical inflection point.

speaker: Todd Jackson
text: The best signal that you have nascent product-market fit is when customers start pulling the product from you. You don't have to push it anymore. They're asking you for it. That's a qualitative signal that data won't show you.
summary: The strongest early signal of product-market fit is when customers start pulling the product from you instead of you pushing it.

speaker: Todd Jackson
text: At the developing stage, the biggest mistake I see is premature scaling. You have a few happy customers and you think you should hire a sales team and start marketing. But you haven't really nailed what makes those customers happy.
summary: The most common product-market fit mistake is premature scaling before deeply understanding what makes early customers successful.

speaker: Claire Vo
text: People often think that I get hired into later stage companies because I'm supposed to teach them how to operate like a big company, and in fact, I say I'm hired to remind them they can operate like a startup.
summary: Experienced operators are hired into later-stage companies not to add process but to remind them they can still operate with startup speed and scrappiness.

speaker: Claire Vo
text: I communicate to my leaders that my expectation is they bring in the clock speed one click faster. If you think something needs to be done this year, it needs to be done this half.
summary: A simple leadership technique for increasing velocity: tell teams to compress every timeline by one increment.

speaker: Claire Vo
text: I'm using CPTO for short code of running product and engineering design functionally together. There should be no debates over what's best for product or what's best for engineering, what's best for design speed. What is best for the organization?
summary: The CPTO model combines product, engineering, and design under one leader, eliminating cross-functional debates in favor of organizational-level optimization.

speaker: Claire Vo
text: Is it going to eliminate PMs next year? Probably not. Are the skills required going to shift? Yes. Could they shift much faster than we all anticipate? Probably.
summary: AI will not eliminate PMs soon but will shift required skills faster than most people anticipate.

speaker: Claire Vo
text: The thing about bending the universe in your favor is it's not about luck. It's about creating as many surface areas for good things to happen as possible. Say yes to things, be visible, put yourself out there, and over time, the opportunities compound.
summary: Creating career luck is about maximizing surface area for serendipity through visibility, saying yes, and letting opportunities compound over time.
